def: Adjusted Gross Income — your total income minus specific deductions like IRA contributions or student loan interest. Used to calculate many tax benefits.
Form your employer sends by January 31 showing wages earned and taxes withheld. You need this to file your 2025 return.
example: Box 1 = taxable wages. Box 2 = federal tax withheld.

def: Form for income NOT from an employer — freelance work, bank interest, dividends, crypto. No taxes withheld, so you may owe more at filing.
example: Client paid you $2,500 for work → they send 1099-NEC.

def: Federal Insurance Contributions Act — 7.65% taken from every paycheck: 6.2% Social Security + 1.45% Medicare. Your employer matches it.
example: On a $4,000 paycheck: $306 goes to FICA taxes.

def: One Big Beautiful Bill — major tax legislation signed July 4, 2025. Raised standard deductions, boosted Child Tax Credit to $2,200, raised SALT cap to $40,000, eliminated tax on qualifying tips and overtime.
example: Affects all 2025 tax returns filed in 2026.
keywords: obbb one big beautiful bill 2025 law

def: Qualified Business Income deduction — allows pass-through business owners (S-Corps, partnerships, sole proprietors) to deduct 20% of qualified business income. Made permanent by OBBB in 2025.
example: $100,000 in QBI → $20,000 deduction → saves ~$4,400 in taxes at 22%.

def: State and Local Tax deduction cap — limits how much state income/property tax you can deduct on your federal return. Was $10,000. Raised to $40,000 for 2025–2029 by OBBB.
example: NY taxpayer paying $35,000 in state taxes can now deduct the full amount.
keywords: salt state local tax deduction cap

def: Retirement account funded with after-tax money. Grows tax-free; withdrawals in retirement are 100% tax-free. 2025 limit: $7,000 ($8,000 if 50+).
example: $7,000 at age 25 could grow to $100,000+ by retirement — all tax-free.

def: Profit from selling an investment. Short-term (under 1 year): taxed as regular income. Long-term (over 1 year): taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20%. 2025: 0% rate applies up to $48,350 single.
example: Bought stock at $10, sold at $25 after 2 years → $15 long-term capital gain.
keywords: capital gains investment profit tax rate

def: Individual Taxpayer Identification Number — 9-digit number (starts with 9) for people who need to file taxes but can't get a Social Security Number. Apply via Form W-7.
example: International student with no SSN → apply for ITIN to file Form 8843.

def: Pass-Through Entity tax election — available in most states, lets an S-Corp or partnership pay state income tax at the entity level. Shareholders/partners get a credit, effectively bypassing the federal SALT cap for business income.
example: Available in 36+ states including NY, CA, IL, NJ, TX, FL.
keywords: pte pass-through entity election salt workaround

def: IRS formula to determine if an international person is a "resident alien" for US taxes. Count: 100% of current year days + 1/3 of last year + 1/6 of two years ago. Need 183+ days total.
example: F-1 students are exempt from this test for their first 5 calendar years in the US.
keywords: substantial presence test nonresident alien international student
